## The signal that makes this account interesting

Harborview sits at the collision of three forces. **First, regulatory**: Washington's RCW 49.19 (passed as HB1162/SB5162 in 2025, in force now, effective 2026-01-01) mandates workplace violence prevention programs in hospitals. Harborview, as a safety-net anchor and teaching hospital, is squarely in scope—and the clock to mandate-readiness is tightening.

**Second, acuity**: High DSH status signals disproportionate patient violence risk. Harborview's ED and behavioral unit serve a population with elevated acuity and WPV incident rates. No facility-specific incident surfaces in our engine (Tier-B news/Tier-C hand-research pending), but the safety-net profile itself is the signal.

**Third, leadership**: An Interim CEO suggests either transition, turnover, or institutional recalibration. This is a window for infrastructure decisions that a permanent exec might defer. Firefly's mandate-readiness playbook—"detect, locate, alert, respond" as a compliance cornerstone—lands differently when the EDM is in flux and the board is watching.

## The play

**Greenfield + mandate-readiness hybrid.** Harborview likely has legacy panic buttons or no integrated WPV detection. The RCW 49.19 mandate creates a 12-month compliance window (effective Jan 2026). Firefly's bundled Ember + Lattice + gunshot detection positions as a single-vendor mandate defense and operational upgrade—especially valuable when an Interim CEO wants to show the board a forward-facing safety posture. Lead with "RCW 49.19 readiness" and "integrated detection + resilience" (the no-IT-dependency angle matters for safety-net networks with legacy IT sprawl).

## Recommended one-off opening

Lead with the mandate + leadership velocity: *"Harborview's RCW 49.19 readiness clock starts Jan 1—and your interim leadership window is the moment to standardize detection across the 413-bed anchor and your network; most safety-net hospitals we work with are 6–8 months out on compliance, and we help you land ahead."*
